origin: Garden hybrid (G. dalmaticum × G. macrorrhizum); 'Biokovo' found wild in Croatia's Biokovo Mountains
habitat: Rocky limestone slopes and open woodland edges of the Dalmatian mountains (parent species' range)
blurb: A vigorous, sterile groundcover cranesbill smothered in pale pink-tinged white flowers with rosy stamens in late spring, over glossy aromatic leaves that carpet the ground and flush red in fall. Tough, weed-proof and deer-proof — the 2015 Perennial Plant of the Year.
sun: Full sun to part shade (takes full shade)
water: Low; drought-tolerant once established, spreads best with occasional summer water
spread: Spreads steadily by rhizomes into a dense, weed-smothering mat; a sterile hybrid, so it never self-sows
seasons: Glossy, aromatic semi-evergreen leaves that flush red in fall and persist through winter; masses of pale pink-tinged white flowers with deep-pink stamens in late spring to early summer
wildlife: Bees and other pollinators work the spring flowers; the aromatic foliage is deer- and rabbit-resistant
deer: Resistant — the aromatic foliage is left alone by deer and rabbits
toxic: Non-toxic; grown as an ornamental groundcover, not a food

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA zone 4 — thoroughly reliable across Boulder and the Front Range (zones 5b–6a) and up into the foothills. The aromatic, glossy foliage is semi-evergreen, persisting through mild winters and flushing red in fall cold.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant from nursery transplants in spring (Apr–May) or early fall (Sep) so roots settle before summer heat or winter. It establishes easily and knits into a solid mat. In containers the aromatic foliage is handsome, but exposed roots are far less cold-hardy than in the ground — sink the pot or shelter it in an unheated garage over winter, and never let it sit waterlogged.[1][2]
care.sun: Grows in full sun through full shade; the densest mat and best bloom come in full sun to part shade. In our intense high-altitude sun a hot, dry site is fine given a little more moisture, and it is one of the better cranesbills for part shade under shrubs.[1][2]
care.soil: Easily grown in average, moderately fertile, well-drained soil; tolerant of chalk, the lean alkaline clay common around Boulder, loam and sand alike. Sharp drainage matters more than fertility — avoid waterlogged ground.[1][2]
care.water: Low. Drought-tolerant once established and content on Boulder's natural precipitation with occasional deep summer soaks; it fills in fastest in moist, humusy soil but does not need it. Water new plantings through the first season.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space transplants 12–18 in apart; they knit together quickly into a solid weed-smothering carpet. Plant at the wider end if you want them to fill in slowly.[1][2]
care.propagation: Because 'Biokovo' is a sterile hybrid it sets no viable seed, so propagate only vegetatively — lift and pull clumps apart or detach rooted rhizome pieces in spring or just after bloom, or take basal cuttings in spring; replant immediately and water in.[1][2]
care.bloom: Masses of five-petaled, very pale pink-tinged white flowers (about 1 in wide) with contrasting deep-pink stamens open in late spring into early summer — roughly May–June on the Front Range — held just above the foliage.[1][2]
care.feeding: None needed. Lean soil keeps the mat dense and well-behaved; skip fertilizer.[2]
care.maintenance: Nearly maintenance-free. Shear the mat lightly after bloom to tidy it and refresh the foliage if you like. The rhizomes spread steadily but are easy to pull back where they overstep — lift the unwanted runners by hand.[1][2]
care.troubles: Essentially pest- and disease-free. The aromatic foliage makes it strongly deer- and rabbit-resistant. The only real failure mode is crown rot in soggy, poorly drained ground — give it drainage and it is bulletproof.[1][2]
care.companions: Pairs with other tough groundcovers and woodland-edge plants — hellebores, hostas, lungwort and ferns — and with spring bulbs, filling bare ground beneath shrubs where it crowds out weeds.[2]

edible.summary: Biokovo cranesbill is grown as an ornamental groundcover, not as a food — don't eat it.[1][2]
edible.caution: Geranium × cantabrigiense is grown for its aromatic foliage and weed-smothering habit, not for eating, and no authoritative source documents an edible use for the plant. It is not listed as notably poisonous by the Royal Horticultural Society or the Missouri Botanical Garden, so treat it as inedible: not a known poison, but not a tested food.[1][2]
